does: This paper provides a comprehensive systematic survey of over 300 recent papers examining how large language models perform reasoning tasks and where they fail. The authors develop a structured taxonomy organizing LLM reasoning research into nine major paradigms: Chain-of-Thought, multi-hop, mathematical, commonsense, visual and temporal, code and algorithmic, retrieval-augmented, tool-augmented agentic, and reinforcement learning-based reasoning. The survey analyzes methodological trends across these paradigms, including prompting strategies and other techniques, while documenting failure modes and inconsistencies in LLM reasoning behavior that arise from sensitivity to task design, model scale, and prompting approaches.
connects: This work builds on the long line of research investigating reasoning capabilities in neural language models, extending from earlier work on structured inference and multi-step problem solving to recent advances in prompting techniques like Chain-of-Thought. It synthesizes research spanning multiple reasoning subdomains—mathematical reasoning, commonsense reasoning, tool use—that have developed somewhat independently, drawing together insights from papers on prompt engineering, scaling laws, and failure analysis. The survey responds to growing recognition that despite impressive benchmark performance, LLMs show fragile and inconsistent reasoning behavior, connecting observations across diverse task formulations and architectural approaches to identify systematic patterns.
enables: This structured taxonomy and analysis enables researchers to identify gaps in current reasoning approaches and prioritize high-impact directions for future work. By cataloging failure modes systematically across reasoning paradigms, it supports the development of more robust reasoning methods and better evaluation frameworks for assessing genuine reasoning capability versus surface-level pattern matching. The organization of the landscape facilitates cross-paradigm transfer of techniques and points toward opportunities for integrating multiple reasoning approaches, supporting efforts to build more reliable and composable reasoning systems in LLMs.
